segments.s380-g89: نگهبان او کرد is a past-tense causative construction: نگهبان (predicate noun 'guard') comes before the verb کرد ('made/caused'), and او is the object pronoun 'him' ('they made him a guard'). پس‌اند is an adverbial particle 'after that/thereafter' that links what follows to this action. مرد here names the person ('the man') (rather than a verbal past of 'to die'), introduced after the adverb as the topic/subject of the following description.
segments.s380-g90: گو before پهلوان‌زاده is an attributive/poetic particle roughly marking 'so-called' or 'the said' (classical usage varies), attaching to the following noun. پهلوان‌زاده is the noun 'son of a champion' and با داغ و درد is a prepositional phrase ('with grief and pain') modifying that man; داغ is a poetic word for burning grief, and درد here denotes emotional sorrow.
segments.s380-g91: بدان here is a fused locative/adverbial ('in/with that') introducing the circumstance ('in that narrowness/distress'). تنگی is the noun 'narrowness/distress'; اندر is another locative preposition 'in/within', used classically for emphasis, and همی is a continuous/time adverb 'still/continually'. زیستی is the 2nd-person singular past/imperfect of زیستن ('you lived' / 'you were living'), so the clause states that 'you lived/continued to live in that distress.'
segments.s380-g92: زمان تا زمان is a classical repetitive expression meaning 'from time to time' or 'occasionally.' زار here functions as an adverbial/intensifying word meaning 'pitiably/miserably' (from lamentation sense), and بگریستی is the 2nd-person singular past of گریستن ('you cried/wailed'), so the clause means 'you wailed miserably at times.'
segments.s380-g81: فرستاده is a participial noun here meaning “the one sent / messenger” (literally ‘sent one’). سوی is a preposition meaning “toward,” followed by the place-name دژ گنبدان (dēzh-e Gonbadān, “the Gonbadān fortress/citadel”) as the destination.
segments.s380-g82: گرفته is a past participle/adjectival form meaning here ‘taken/held’ or more idiomatically ‘held/surrounded’; it describes the state produced. پس و پیش is an idiomatic pair meaning “front and back / around,” and اسپهبدان is the plural agent ‘the spahbeds (army commanders)’; the phrase therefore means that the spahbeds were positioned around (him).
segments.s380-g83: پر از درد is a descriptive phrase: پر ‘full (of)’ + از used as ‘of’ (پرِ ازِ درد = ‘full of pain’). بردند is the past tense verb ‘they carried/took’; بر + کوهسار marks the destination (‘onto the mountain-side’), with بر functioning as the locative/prepositional prefix.
segments.s380-g84: ستون آوریدند is a simple past clause: ستون ‘pillar(s)’ (object) + آوریدند ‘they brought’. ز آهن uses poetic z for ‘of/from’ to mark material (‘of iron’); the numeral چهار (four) logically modifies ستون but appears after the material (typical older word order: noun + material + numeral).
segments.s380-g85: به کرده + noun phrase is an adverbial/participial construction: به + کرده (past participle ‘having made’ / ‘made’) introduces the resultive state. ستونها بزرگ آهنین is the noun phrase ‘the pillars, great, iron’ (noun + adjectives); note the adjective order is postpositive, as usual in classical Persian.
segments.s380-g86: This is a spatial description: سر ‘head/top’ اندر ‘in/within’ هوا ‘the air’ — and بن ‘base/bottom’ اندر زمین ‘in the earth’. اندر is the older preposition for ‘in’, and the clause states that the pillars’ tops were in the air while their bases were fixed in the ground.
segments.s380-g87: مر here is an archaic/poetic element (not matching modern pronoun forms) and appears before او را; او را is the prototypical object phrase ‘him’ with را marking the definite object. برانجا means ‘at that place / there’ and ببستند is the past tense ‘they bound/tied’; سخت modifies the verb adverbially (‘firmly, severely’).
segments.s380-g88: ز تختش بیفگند is a past action: ز (poetic ‘from’) + تختش ‘his throne/bed’ (possessive suffix) + بیفگند ‘they threw/cast (him)’. The following و links the consequence phrase برگشت بخت, where برگشت is a noun/verb meaning ‘reversal / (it) turned back’ and بخت ‘fortune/fate’ — together ‘his fortune was reversed / luck turned’.
segments.s380-g73: چو here is the conjunction 'when' (not 'as' or 'if'), introducing a temporal clause. کردند is the past plural verb 'they did/made' and takes the following noun phrase زنجیر در گردنش ('a chain in/around his neck') as its object; گردن+ش is the head noun 'neck' with the enclitic possessive ش = 'his'. در is used spatially ('in/around') and links زنجیر to گردن.
segments.s380-g74: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered' (classical register). بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound,tied' describing what was to be done. به marks direction/purpose 'to/into', در here names the place entered ('the door/gate' ≈ prison), and بردنش is بردن + -ش (him) 'to take him' with infinitival/nominal sense forming the commanded action.
segments.s380-g75: بیارید is an imperative plural 'bring (here)!' (the annotated lemma/senses list seems mismatched to the surface form, so the supplied senses do not fit this occurrence). گفتا is the archaic/narrative past 'he/they said' placed after the imperative to report speech. یکی modifies پیل نر as 'a certain/a single male elephant' (a indefinite 'one').
segments.s380-g76: دونده and پرنده are attributive/adjectival here, characterizing the elephant as 'swift' (دونده) and 'flying' or 'winged' (پرنده) — poetic hyperbole. چو is the simile marker 'like/as', مرغی is 'like a bird' (adjectival), به is the preposition/adverbial particle forming the phrase به پر 'on/with a wing', and پر is 'feather/wing'.
segments.s380-g77: فراز is used adverbially 'up/on high' modifying آوریدند 'they brought' (so فراز آوریدند = 'they brought up/aloft'). پیلی is descriptive/indefinite for an elephant (the annotation's senses are limited; here it functions as 'an elephant'), چو introduces a simile and نیل names the Nile—a color/image simile 'as dark as the Nile'.
segments.s380-g78: مر in the given senses list does not match the form/function here (it functions as a particle/pronoun in the line to mark the person bound) so its listed senses are inapplicable; او is the third-person pronoun 'him', را is the definite object marker, ببستند is the past plural 'they bound/tied', بر + پشت = 'onto the back of', and پیل is 'elephant' — together 'they bound him on the elephant's back.'
segments.s380-g79: چو is the temporal 'when'. بردندش is بردند + -ش 'they took him/carried him away' (past plural with enclitic object). از پیش functions as the prepositional phrase 'from before/in the presence of' and فرخ پدر here is a name or title ('Farrokh the father' — functioning as the person before whom he was taken).
segments.s380-g80: دیده is the noun 'eye' (here pluralized by the numeral دو preceding it) and پر ... از is the idiom 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'), so پر از آب = 'full of water' i.e. eyes full of tears. و simply coordinates, and رخساره‌تر is the comparative form of رخساره ('countenance/complexion') with -تر 'more', meaning 'his face/mien became more expressive/changed' (poetic compressed comparative).
segments.s380-g41: This is an interrogative phrase: چه (question word) is fronted, گویید is a present (here addressing the elders: 'do you say') and پیران is the addressee/subject 'elders' (postverbal subject is common in classical Persian). که introduces a subordinate/relative sense 'about/regarding', followed by the prepositional phrase با این پسر 'with/about this son.'
segments.s380-g42: This is an exclamative evaluation: چه نیکو (literally 'how good/excellent') modifies the clause; بود is the past copula 'was'. کار کردن is a noun+infinitive construction meaning 'the doing/work' or 'the deed performed', with کردن as the verbal noun, and پدر is the agent 'the father.' Word order places the evaluative phrase before the copula and the action phrase after it.
segments.s380-g43: گزینانش is 'his chosen ones' with the 3rd-person possessive enclitic -ش; گفتند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they said' (reporting speech). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address a sovereign, and شهریار is the addressee 'king.' The sequence reports what his men said to the king.
segments.s380-g44: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive (here 'should not come' / 'must not be counted'); خود این 'this very one' uses خود as an intensifier ('himself/this very'); هرگز 'never' negates; اندر شمار is a fixed prepositional phrase 'into the count/among the number.' Word order places the negative verb before its complements in classical style.
segments.s380-g45: This is a paired nominal phrase: 'پدر زنده' ('the father alive') and 'پور جویای گاه' ('the son seeking a time/opportunity'). جویای is a participial adjective 'seeking' that takes گاه ('time' / 'occasion') as its object; the coordinate ו links the two descriptions.
segments.s380-g46: ازین خام‌تر is a comparative phrase 'more naive/foolish than this' (از + این, with -تر comparative on خام). نیز here intensifies as 'even/also.' کاری is the noun 'a deed/any action' functioning as the object of the negative command مخواه, where مخواه is an old/formal negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not desire/attempt.'
segments.s380-g47: جهاندار گفتا is a narratorial clause: جهاندار 'the sovereign' + گفتا, an archaic past form 'said.' که introduces the reported content; اینک 'now' or 'behold' is emphatic and identifies the son being pointed out.
segments.s380-g48: This is a relative clause describing the son: آهنگ دارد is a present-tense construction 'has an intention / intends' (آهنگ = intention/aim, دارد = 'has'), and به جای پدر is an idiom meaning 'in place of the father' or 'to succeed the father.' The relative pronoun که links this clause to 'پسر.'
segments.s380-g33: پسر را places ‘the son’ with the enclitic را to mark the beneficiary/definite topic (here: ‘to/for the son’). The three nouns جهان و درفش و سپاه are a coordinated list (joined by و) that follow that marked beneficiary and are the things assigned to him.
segments.s380-g34: پدر را functions like the previous phrase: ‘to/for the father’ with را marking the beneficiary/object. یکی modifies the following nouns as a numeral ‘one’; تاج و زرین کلاه is a coordination where زرین (golden) is an adjective before کلاه (cap/hat).
segments.s380-g35: نباشد is a negative existential/optative form of “to be” (here: ‘there is not’ / ‘there is no’). بران is a deictic ‘on/for that’ (referring back to the person just mentioned); پور is the noun ‘son/descendant’; همداستان is an adjective meaning ‘like‑minded’ or ‘of the same mind’ describing پور.
segments.s380-g36: پسندند is 3rd‑person plural present of پسندیدن ‘to approve/consider fitting’ (here: ‘they deem fitting’). گردان is used in a verbal/nominal sense meaning ‘to fashion/turn’ (poetic usage: ‘to compose/turn [the tale]’). چنین داستان is the object phrase ‘such a story/this tale’ (چنین = thus/such; داستان = tale).
segments.s380-g37: ز بهر is a prepositional phrase ‘for the sake of / because of’ (ز = ‘from/because of’ in poetic use; بهر = ‘for/for the sake of’). یکی modifies the following nouns as the numeral ‘one’; تاج و افسر is a coordinated noun phrase ‘a crown and a helmet’; پسر follows to mark the helmet as belonging to or intended for the son (genitive relation).
segments.s380-g38: تن ... را is a definite direct object (را marking ‘the body’ as the thing affected). باب is the noun following تن (here a proper/identified noun in apposition or name); دور خواهد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘will make/remove (away)’, with دور ‘away/removed’ and خواهد the auxiliary ‘will/shall’. ز سر is a prepositional complement ‘from the head’ (z = from; سر = head), describing the separation.
segments.s380-g39: کند is the simple verb ‘does/makes’ (here narrative present: ‘he marshals/sets in motion’). با سپاهش is a prepositional phrase ‘with his army’ where سپاه + ش (enclitic) = ‘his army’. پس is an adverb ‘then/afterwards’. آهنگ here means ‘aim/intention’ or ‘direction (of march)’ and اوی is the enclitic/old form ‘of him’ (i.e. ‘toward him’).
segments.s380-g40: نهاده is a perfect participle ‘having placed/put’ (here ‘has set’). دلش is ‘his heart’ (دل + ش). نیز is ‘also/likewise’. بر جنگ اوی is a prepositional phrase ‘upon/for war against him’ (بر = on/against; جنگ = fight/war; اوی = him), i.e. his heart is set on waging war against that man.
segments.s380-g17: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle marking a continuing or habitual action ('continually'). داردش is دارد (3rd‑person singular 'has/keeps') with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('him/it'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that') and is followed by شود (subjunctive/optative 'becomes') and the adjective چیره‌دست ('skilful'), so the whole segment means 'she keeps him continually so that he becomes skilful.'
segments.s380-g18: بیاموزدش is the verb بیاموختن in 3rd‑person present with the enclitic -ش ('teaches him'). The following infinitive خوردن functions as the verb's object ('to eat'). و simply links the two infinitival/resulting actions. بر نشست is a verb phrase with preposition بر + نشستن ('to sit on / sit up'); here it pairs with the preceding infinitive to express the things he is taught ('to eat and to sit up').
segments.s380-g19: بسی here functions as an adverb meaning 'much/very' modifying the noun phrase رنج بیند. رنج is the noun 'suffering/hardship' and بیند is the (archaic/classical) present of دیدن used in the sense 'experiences/undergoes.' گرانمایه is an attributive adjective 'precious/esteemed' placed before مرد ('man'); together 'گرانمایه مرد' = 'the noble/esteemed man.'
segments.s380-g20: سورای is a noun 'feast/revelry' in the nominative position. کندش is the verb کردن in present with the enclitic object -ش ('does/makes for him' or 'holds for him'); the object pronoun refers back to the man. آزموده is a past participle/adjective 'proven/experienced' modifying نبرد ('battle'), so آزموده نبرد describes being 'battle‑tested' or 'experienced in battle.' Word order places the participle before the noun it qualifies in this poetic diction.
segments.s380-g21: چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). آزاده را is a classical/genitive construction in which را follows the possessor (آزاده) to form 'the noble's/ the free one's' (the را here does not mark a direct object but helps form the noun phrase). ره 'way/path' is the possessed noun, به + مردی 'to/at a man' marks the target, and رسد (رسیدن) is a present tense verb 'reaches/attains.' The clause therefore means 'when the noble's path reaches a man.'
segments.s380-g22: چنان زر begins a simile 'such/similarly, gold...' and که introduces a relative/comparative clause ('as/that'). از کان uses از + کان where کان here means 'the mine/ore,' i.e. 'from the mine.' به زردی رسد uses به + noun + رسد ('attains/comes to yellowness'), so the clause compares the man to gold that from the mine attains its yellow color. The past participle/order is poetic but the syntax is straightforward: comparative clause introduced by که.
segments.s380-g23: مراورا is a fused pronoun phrase meaning 'me and him' (object form). بجوید is the present tense of جستن ('he/they seek'); چو means 'as/like' here, and جویندگان is the agent noun 'seekers.' The segment reads 'he seeks me and him like seekers (do),' with the simile introduced by چو.
segments.s380-g24: ورا is an object/locative pronoun 'of him/to him' or 'about him' and is the topic of the following verb. بیش is an adverb 'more' (i.e. 'further/more at length'). گویند is the verb 'they say' (here represented by the given lemma form) and گویندگان is the agent noun 'speakers/narrators' placed after the verb for emphasis; together the line means 'and the narrators say more about him.'
segments.s380-g65: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of' (به = 'to/for', پیش = 'in the presence of') placed before the verb for emphasis. آوریدند is a third-person plural past verb 'they brought'; آهنگران is the plural agent/subject 'the blacksmiths' matching the verb. The word order (prep phrase + verb + subject) is a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s380-g66: The three nouns are linked by و ('and') forming a coordinated list: غل (fetter), بند (bond/fetter), زنجیرهای (chains, plural). The adjective گران follows the list and qualifies them as 'heavy/weighty' (postnominal adjectival placement is common in classical Persian).
segments.s380-g67: دران انجمن is a locative phrase 'in that assembly' (دران = 'in that', انجمن = 'assembly'). کس here, in combination with the negative verb that follows, functions as 'no one'. به + خواهش marks purpose/intent 'to (make) a request', and زبان = 'tongue/speech' means 'utterance'—together 'to move the tongue to request'.
segments.s380-g68: نجنبید is the negative past form of جنبیدن meaning 'did not move/stir' (here 'did not speak/move the tongue'). بر شهریار جهان is a prepositional phrase 'before/at the sovereign (شهریار) of the world (جهان)', with بر indicating direction/position toward someone. The clause as a whole: 'no one moved a tongue to plead before the king of the world.'
segments.s380-g69: ببستند is the third-person plural past 'they bound/tied'. او را is the definite direct object ('him' + object marker را). The following nouns سر و دست و پای enumerate the body parts bound (head, hands, and feet). The verb precedes the object phrase here, a possible poetic word order in classical Persian.
segments.s380-g70: به پیش is the locative/prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of' (به = 'to/for', پیش = 'in the presence of') attached to the binding action. جهاندار گیهان خدای are stacked titles in apposition: 'sovereign/holder of the world (جهاندار گیهان)' and 'khodā/“God”' as an honorific title for the ruler. The sequence identifies before whom he was bound.
segments.s380-g71: چنانش is چنان 'thus/so' plus the enclitic -ش referring back to 'him' (object clitic), meaning 'them/him thus'. ببستند 'they bound' is the verb; پای استوار is 'a/ the firm/secure foot' (پای + adjective استوار), i.e. 'his feet were bound firmly.' The clitic signals the object without repeating او.
segments.s380-g72: که introduces a result clause meaning 'so that' (here giving the consequence). هرکش = 'whoever/anyone'; همی is the old habitual/progressive particle giving generality ('whenever/whoever'); دید = 'saw/beheld'; بگریست = 'would weep/weep' (past form used in result clause); زار is adverbial/adjunct 'miserably/piteously'. Together: 'so that anyone who saw (him) wept piteously.'
segments.s380-g25: This clause has an inverted, compact poetic order: noun + verb + predicates. 'سواری' is a predicate noun 'a rider/horseman', and 'شود' is the stative verb 'becomes' (present/subjunctive/habitual sense). 'نیک' modifies 'سواری' ('a good/fine horseman'), while 'پیروز رزم' is an adjectival phrase 'victorious in battle' (پیروز followed by its domain رزم).
segments.s380-g26: This is a short circumstantial phrase: 'سرانجمنها' ('the heads/leaders of the assembly') followed by two prepositional phrases introduced by repeated 'به' marking the spheres where they appear. Here 'به ... رزم' and 'به ... بزم' mean 'in/at war' and 'in/at the feast' respectively; the repeated 'به' is a poetic parallelism.
segments.s380-g27: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'نیرو' here means a body of troops/force, and 'کند' is the verb 'makes/assembles' (do/make). 'با' introduces the company/qualities brought with that force; the three nouns 'سرو یال و شاخ' are a poetic list of images (cypress, mane, horn) — compact metaphors describing the appearance or banners/horsemen that accompany the force.
segments.s380-g28: This clause describes the father's state: 'پدر' is the subject/topic, 'پیر گشته' is a compound participial phrase 'having become old' (گشته = become/turned), and 'نشسته به کاخ' places him 'seated in the palace'. The participles stack to give his resultant condition ('grown old and sitting').
segments.s380-g29: Here 'جهان را' marks 'the world' as the direct object (را as definite/object marker). 'کند' is the active verb 'makes'; 'یکسره' = 'completely/entirely'. 'زو' (poetic form of 'از او') means 'of/from him', and 'تهی' means 'devoid/empty (of)'. The whole means 'he makes the world entirely empty of him' (i.e., leaves no one else or makes the world bereft).
segments.s380-g30: 'نباشد' is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'بودن' used here as a predicative 'is not / there is not'. It negates the following predicate 'سزاوار تخت مهی' — 'worthy/deserving of the moon‑like (مهی) throne (تخت)'. In short: '(there) is no one deserving of the moon‑like throne.'
segments.s380-g31: 'ندارد' is the negative of 'داشتن' — 'does not have/has not'. 'پدر' is the topic/subject. 'جز' gives exclusion 'except', so 'ندارد پدر جز یکی نام تخت' reads literally 'the father has, except one, (a) throne-name' → 'the father has only one name of the throne.' Word order places the negated verb before the subject, a common Persian poetic order.
segments.s380-g32: 'نشسته' is a participle 'seated'; 'در ایوان' is the locative 'in the iwan/porch'. 'نگهبان رخت' is a noun phrase 'guardian/keeper of (his) rakh(t)' — here 'رخت' means clothing/gear/baggage. Together: 'seated in the porch, (he is) the guardian of his garments.'
segments.s380-g49: ولیکن is an adversative conjunction ('but'). The clause follows a typical Persian SOV order: subject من (I) + object او را (him, with را as the definite object marker) + instrument phrase به چوبی (with a stick/club, where به marks the instrument) + verb زنم at the end. زنم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/jussive form of زدن used here to express intention or determination ('I will strike' / 'I shall beat').
segments.s380-g50: که introduces a result/purpose clause here ('so that' / 'such that'). گیرند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive ('they may seize/take' or 'they may take' as a lesson). عبرت همه is the object phrase 'a lesson for all / everyone's lesson' with همه quantifying عبرت. برزنم is an adverbial/place expression 'in the public square / village threshing-floor' (an open communal place) modifying the clause; word order places the verb earlier and the place after the object, typical in poetry.
segments.s380-g51: ببندم is 1st-person singular jussive/present-subjunctive of بستن used here to state the speaker's resolve ('I will bind/fasten'). چنانش is چنان + the enclitic ش (so/such + 'him' or 'it so'), meaning 'him thus' or 'in such a way'; سزاوار is a predicate meaning 'deserving' or 'as is due'. پس is a connective 'then/thereupon'.
segments.s380-g52: ببندی here is a jussive/imperative form ('bind' / 'fasten (him)') — in context it continues the speaker's instruction or determination. که introduces a result or comparative clause ('so that' / 'such that'). کس را is 'no one / anyone' with را marking the object. نبستست is a past negative passive/perfect construction meaning 'was not fastened' or 'has not been bound' (used comparatively: 'so that none has ever bound [anyone] (thus)'). The final کس repeats 'anyone' for emphasis; the clause is elliptical and poetic.
segments.s380-g53: پسر گفت is a simple past narrative ('the son said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly; here it introduces an address to شاه آزاده‌خوی ('the magnanimous king'), where آزاده‌خوی is an adjectival epithet meaning high‑minded or magnanimous. Word order is vocative + title + epithet.
segments.s380-g54: This clause is a rhetorical question. The surface tokens are split oddly (م + را) in the provided segmentation, but syntactically it functions as 'مرا ... کی کند آرزوی' = 'When / who would make me wish ...' مرگ تو is 'your death' (the thing that might be wished for). کی here functions rhetorically as 'ever / when would' and کند is the present/future auxiliary/verb 'would do/make'; آرزوی (here a noun) with کردن implied yields 'to wish for'. The object/experiencer مرا is fronted for emphasis ('for me / I'), so the whole means 'When would I wish for your death?'
segments.s380-g55: ندانم is the negative present of دانستن ('I do not know'). گناهی is an indefinite noun 'any sin' (گناه + ی). من‌ای here is the speaker's 'I' followed by the vocative particle ای addressed to شهریار ('O king'): the phrase reads as an address 'O king' after the initial denial; in classical Persian vocatives and pronouns can appear in this order.
segments.s380-g56: که introduces a relative clause modifying گناهی ('a sin that ...'). کردستم is an archaic/formal past/perfect first‑person form ('I did / I have done') meaning 'I have committed'. اندر همه روزگار is a prepositional/time phrase 'in/throughout all (my) days' (اندر = in; روزگار = time, life, days). Together: 'any sin that I have done in all my time.'
segments.s380-g9: شه خسروان is a titulary nominal phrase ('the king of kings'); گفت is the past narrative verb 'said/spoke' governing the following prepositional phrase با موبدان 'with/to the mobeds' (موبدان is the plural of موبد, Zoroastrian priests). Word order is Subject–Verb–(with + addressee).
segments.s380-g10: بدان here breaks into ب + دان: ب functions as a prepositional/prefixal 'to/for', and دان is a demonstrative/anaphoric element ('that/those')—together giving 'to those'. The phrase names the people addressed: رادمردان 'noblemen, men of integrity' and اسپهبدان 'spahbeds/army commanders'.
segments.s380-g11: چه گویید is a present interrogative phrase 'What do you say? / What are you saying?'. گفتا is a narrative parenthetical 'he said' (classical/poetic past marker inserted), and که introduces the content clause 'that you are noble' (آزاده‌اید = you are free/noble). Note that آزاده‌اید is second-person plural present (you are noble).
segments.s380-g12: به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship / severely' (به + سختی). همه modifies the following noun phrase and means 'all/everyone'. پرورش داده‌اید is the compound verb 'you have given upbringing / you have reared' (perfective sense, 2nd pl.); contextually this reads as 'you have (all) been/been reared' or more naturally 'you have all been brought up (with hardship)' or 'you have reared (him/them) with hardship' depending on the implied object.
segments.s380-g13: به گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). کسی را که باشد پسر is a relative/hypothetical clause 'whoever (کسی) is/has a son' — را is topical/object marker attached to کسی, که introduces the clause, and باشد is a third‑person form used here in a general/hypothetical sense ('may be / is').
segments.s380-g14: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/for him'. شاد باشد is a predicative phrase 'may be glad / will be glad' (شاد = happy/glad; باشد = third-person present/subjunctive/optative used to state resulting state). دل تاجور is the subject phrase 'the merchant's heart' (دل = heart, تاجور = merchant); word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'may the merchant's heart be glad for him.'
segments.s380-g15: به هنگام شیرین is a temporal phrase 'at the time of Shirin' (به هنگام = at the time of; شیرین here is a proper name). به دایه دهد is 'he gives to the nurse' (دهد is 3rd sing. of دادن, used in narrative; دایه = nurse). The verb is in the narrative/present form often used in epic storytelling.
segments.s380-g16: یکی modifies تاج (one/a certain crown). زرینش is 'his/her golden' (زرین = golden; -ش suffix = his/her/its), so تاج زرینش = 'his/her golden crown'. بر سر نهد is the verbal phrase 'places/sets on (the) head' (بر سر = on the head; نَهَد = نهاد, to place, 3rd sing.), describing the action of putting the crown on the nurse's head.
segments.s380-g57: به جان تو is a common oath formula meaning 'by your life' (به + جان = 'by the life/of'); ای شاه is a vocative 'O king'. Here شاه‌گر should be read as شاه + گر, where گر = 'if' (conditional), so the sequence marks a conditional clause ('O king, if ...'). بد به دل means 'an evil/sinister thing into the heart' — bad (بد) placed with the prepositional phrase به دل 'in/into the heart'.
segments.s380-g58: گمان برده‌ام is a perfect construction 'I have taken/harbored a suspicion' (برده‌ام = برده + ام, 'have taken/I have'). پس here is a consequence connector 'then/so'. The verbless clause سرم بر گسل ('my head upon the fissure/rock') is elliptical: the consequence is stated without an explicit verb, i.e. 'then let my head be on the rock' (an implied punitive result). بر is the preposition 'upon.'
segments.s380-g59: ولیکن is an adversative 'but/yet'. تو شاهی is a nominative phrase 'you are king' with the copula omitted (typical in Persian). و فرمان تراست is a coordinated predicate: فرمان 'the command' + تراست, an archaic fused form meaning 'is yours' ('تـَ + راست' = 'it belongs to you'), so 'the command is yours.'
segments.s380-g60: تراام من is an emphatic, slightly archaic word order meaning 'I am yours' (ترا = 'to you', ام = 'I am', followed by من for emphasis). The following و بند و زندان تراست lists objects (بند, زندان) followed by تراست 'are yours', i.e. 'and fetters and prison belong to you.'
segments.s380-g61: کنون is the adverb 'now'. بند فرما is an imperative request/command: بند (fetter/bind) + فرما (an honorific/imperative verb 'do/command') = 'bind (me) now.' و‌گر 'and if' introduces a conditional; خواه is 'you wish/if you will' and کش is an imperative form (here understood as 'kill/slay' or 'stretch/drag' in context — an alternative punitive action). The clause offers an alternative: 'bind me, or if you prefer, kill me.'
segments.s380-g62: مرا دل درستست is a verbless copular statement with an unusual word order: مرا (the accusative/possessive form 'me/my') + دل (heart) + درستست (درست + است 'is correct/steady') = 'my heart is steadfast.' و آهسته هش adds an adverb (آهسته 'softly/quietly') and هش, an interjection/exclamation ('hush!' or 'beware!' in older usage) — here a quieting admonition.
segments.s380-g63: سر خسروان گفت is 'the chief (سر) of the kings (خسروان) said' — subject + genitive + past verb. بند آورید is an imperative plural 'bring fetters' (بند = fetter, آورید = bring). The verb آورید is the polite/immediate imperative 'bring.'
segments.s380-g64: مر او را ببندید is a command 'bind him to me' with مر = 'to me' (dative), او را = 'him' (object + را), and ببندید the imperative 'bind.' و introduces a second command: زین مگذرید literally 'do not let the saddle pass' — idiomatically 'do not spare the saddle' or 'do not allow him to go free with his saddle' (مگذار + ید negative imperative 'do not let/pass').
segments.s380-g1: چو at the start is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a past-tense clause 'became aware' (آگاه = aware; شد = became). کامد is an archaic past form meaning 'came/arrived' and here appears before its noun پسر (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian). The whole segment reads 'When the king became aware, the son came...'.
segments.s380-g2: کلاه کیان is a possessive noun phrase 'the crown of the Kians' (کیان = the royal/Kayan house). بر نهاده is from نهادن ('to place') in past/past-participial form 'placed/put on'; the preposition بر + بسر (archaic fused form of بر سر) means 'upon the head'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm: object (کلاه کیان) then verb phrase (بر نهاده) plus locative (بسر = 'on the head').
segments.s380-g3: مهان و کهانرا همه خواند پیش: خواند here means 'he called/summoned'. کهانرا carries the object marker را and refers to 'the world/ages' or 'the great ones' as a collective; پیش means 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. he called them up before him). The sequence places the objects before the verb for poetic order.
segments.s380-g4: همه زند و استا به نزدیک خویش: two verbs joined by و describe what he did with 'everyone'. زند here is a poetic/archaic verbal form (not the Zand commentary) used to mean he set or arranged them; استا is from ایستادن 'to stand' (here 'set/caused to stand/stand in order'). به نزدیک خویش is an adverbial phrase 'close to himself' (خویش = his own). Together: he arranged/placed everyone near him (seated/standing them).
segments.s380-g5: همه موبدان را به کرسی نشاند: همه موبدان را is the definite object ('all the mobeds' marked by را). به کرسی نشاند uses نشاند (past of نشاندن) meaning 'set/caused to sit' and کرسی 'seat/throne' as the place where they were seated. The structure is 'he seated all the mobeds on the throne.'
segments.s380-g6: پس آن خسرو تیغ‌زن را بخواند: پس = 'then'. آن خسرو تیغ‌زن را is the object 'that Khosrow, the sword-striker/swordsman' (را marks it definite). بخواند here functions as a narrative past 'he called/summoned' (classical epic style uses this form).
segments.s380-g7: بیامد گو is a common epic construction meaning 'he came, saying' (گو used as a verbal adverb 'saying/so speaking'). دست کرده is a perfect-participial phrase 'having made/placed his hand' (i.e. having taken hold or readied his hand), and بکش is an imperative/hortative form 'draw/strike/pull' used here as reported speech or a sudden action in narration ('he drew'). Together they describe his approach and immediate action of seizing/drawing.
segments.s380-g8: به پیش پدر شد پرستار فش: به پیش پدر = 'in front of/before his father'. شد is the copular/past 'became'. پرستار is a predicate noun 'caretaker/attendant' or 'worshipper' depending on context (here 'caretaker/attendant'). فش is an archaic noun (contextually an item/office connected to arms or a charge) whose precise sense is uncertain in the supplied senses. Grammatically the phrase means 'he became the keeper/attendant of the fsh (in the presence of his father)'.

sentences.s380-s45: They made him his guard; afterwards the man — the champion's son — was left with grief and pain.
sentences.s380-s46: In that hardship you continued to live; from time to time you wailed miserably.
sentences.s380-s41: They sent a messenger toward the Gonbadān fortress, and the spahbeds closed in around (him), front and back.
sentences.s380-s42: They carried him, full of pain, up onto the mountain-side, and they brought four iron pillars.
sentences.s380-s43: They had made large iron pillars — their heads in the air and their bases in the earth.
sentences.s380-s44: They bound him fast there; they threw him from his throne, and his fortune turned (was reversed).
sentences.s380-s37: When they put a chain around his neck, he commanded that he be bound and taken into the prison.
sentences.s380-s38: They cried, 'Bring a male elephant!' — a swift, flying one like a bird on a wing.
sentences.s380-s39: They brought up an elephant, dark as the Nile; they bound him onto the elephant's back.
sentences.s380-s40: When they took him from before Farrokh the father, his two eyes were full of water and his countenance even more expressive.
sentences.s380-s21: What do you elders say about this son — how excellent was the father's deed?
sentences.s380-s22: His chosen men said, 'O king, this one should never himself be counted among them.'
sentences.s380-s23: With the father alive and the son seeking an opportunity, do not attempt any deed more foolish than this.
sentences.s380-s24: The sovereign said: 'Behold this son who intends to take his father's place.'
sentences.s380-s17: To the son: the world, the banner, and the army; to the father: one crown and a golden cap.
sentences.s380-s18: There is no like-minded son for that man; they deem it fitting to fashion/tell the tale in this way.
sentences.s380-s19: For the sake of one crown and a helmet for the son, he will take the father’s body away from (remove it from) the head.
sentences.s380-s20: He marshals his army and then turns his intent toward him; he has likewise set his heart on fighting him.
sentences.s380-s9: She continually keeps him so that he may become skillful; she teaches him to eat and to sit up.
sentences.s380-s10: The noble man endures many hardships; a feast is given for him, (he is) tried in battle.
sentences.s380-s11: When the noble's path reaches a man, he is like gold that comes from the mine and attains its yellow hue.
sentences.s380-s12: He seeks me and him like seekers do, and the narrators say more about him.
sentences.s380-s33: The blacksmiths brought forward heavy fetters, bonds, and chains.
sentences.s380-s34: In that assembly no one moved his tongue to beg before the sovereign of the world.
sentences.s380-s35: They bound him by head and hands and feet before the sovereign, lord of the world, God.
sentences.s380-s36: They bound his feet so firmly that anyone who saw him would weep piteously.
sentences.s380-s13: He becomes a fine horseman and victorious in battle; leaders of the assemblies (are) in war and at feasts.
sentences.s380-s14: When he marshals a force with cypress, mane and horn, the father, grown old, sits in the palace.
sentences.s380-s15: He makes the world entirely empty of him; there is no one fit for the moon‑like throne.
sentences.s380-s16: The father has nothing except one name of the throne; seated in the porch, he is the guardian of his garments.
sentences.s380-s25: But I will strike him with a club so that everyone may take warning in the public square.
sentences.s380-s26: I will bind him thus as he deserves; then bind him so that none has ever been bound so before.
sentences.s380-s27: The son said, 'O magnanimous king, when would I ever wish for your death?'
sentences.s380-s28: I do not know, O king, of any sin that I have committed in all my days.
sentences.s380-s5: The king of kings spoke to the mobeds, (addressing) those noblemen and spahbeds.
sentences.s380-s6: He said, 'What do you say — that you are noble? You have all been raised with severity.'
sentences.s380-s7: In the world, whoever has a son, the merchant's heart will be glad for him.
sentences.s380-s8: When (it was) Shirin's time, he gave to the nurse one golden crown and set it upon her head.
sentences.s380-s29: By your life, O king — if I have harbored an evil suspicion in my heart, then let my head be (laid) upon the rock.
sentences.s380-s30: But you are king and the command is yours; I am yours, and fetters and prison are yours.
sentences.s380-s31: Now bind (me), and if you will, kill (me); my heart is firm — and softly, hush!
sentences.s380-s32: The chief of the kings said, 'Bring fetters!' — 'Bind him to me, and do not spare the saddle.'
sentences.s380-s1: When the king became aware, the son came and set the Kian crown upon his head.
sentences.s380-s2: He summoned all the guests and the world before him; he placed everyone near himself, seating and setting them in order.
sentences.s380-s3: He seated all the mobeds on the throne; then he called that swordsman Khosrow.
sentences.s380-s4: He came, saying so, and, having put his hand ready, drew (his weapon); before his father he became the keeper/attendant of the fesh.
